attributes.stem:
A nine-month-old boy presents to the General Practice clinic with his third episode of otitis media in two months. He has also had two episodes of pneumonia requiring hospital admission since six months of age. He was well for the first six months of life. He has no other medical history and takes no regular medications. His immunisations are up to date. His parent mentions that a maternal uncle died in early childhood from recurrent infections.

On examination, he appears well between episodes of infection. His vital signs are within normal limits. There is reduced air entry at the right lung base. Tonsils and lymph nodes are small. Blood tests reveal:

  • Immunoglobulin G: 0.5 g/L (3.0-10.0)

  • Immunoglobulin A: <0.1 g/L (0.3-1.2)

  • Immunoglobulin M: <0.1 g/L (0.4-2.0)

  • CD19+ B cells: 0% (5-20)
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This case presents classic features of X-linked agammaglobulinaemia (XLA). The patient is male, developed symptoms after six months of age (when maternal antibodies wane), has recurrent bacterial infections, and has a family history suggestive of X-linked inheritance. The laboratory findings of absent B cells and very low immunoglobulins are diagnostic.

X-linked agammaglobulinaemia is caused by mutations in the Bruton tyrosine kinase (BTK) gene, which is essential for B cell development. This results in a complete absence of mature B cells and, consequently, very low or absent immunoglobulins of all classes. Treatment involves regular immunoglobulin replacement therapy.

Common variable immunodeficiency typically presents later in childhood or early adulthood. While it also causes hypogammaglobulinaemia, B cells are usually present but dysfunctional.

Selective IgA deficiency involves isolated absence of IgA with normal levels of other immunoglobulin classes. It often remains asymptomatic and would not cause such severe recurrent infections.

IgG4-related disease is a chronic inflammatory condition characterised by tissue infiltration with IgG4-positive plasma cells, typically presenting in adults with organ enlargement and fibrosis. It would not present with recurrent infections in infancy.

Combined immunodeficiency affects both B and T cell immunity and typically presents earlier with more severe infections, including opportunistic pathogens. Growth failure and chronic diarrhoea are common features.
learningPoints: X-linked agammaglobulinaemia presents in male infants after six months of age when maternal antibodies wane.
Recurrent bacterial infections, particularly of the respiratory tract, are the hallmark of X-linked agammaglobulinaemia.
Absence of B cells and very low immunoglobulin levels are diagnostic features of X-linked agammaglobulinaemia.
references: El-Sayed ZA, Abramova I, Aldave JC, et al. X-linked agammaglobulinemia (XLA):Phenotype, diagnosis, and therapeutic challenges around the world. World Allergy Organ J. 2019;12(3):100018. Published 2019 Mar 22. doi:10.1016/j.waojou.2019.100018
